event_blocks.summary: The Men's Pole Vault Final commenced in an indoor athletics stadium, with the overhead scoreboard displaying 'IT'S FINALS TIME!' and 'WORLD CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22' as an announcer declared the event. The stadium transitioned to a darkened, atmospheric setting illuminated by purple and blue stage lighting, featuring animated pole vaulter silhouettes on large vertical screens and scrolling text for 'WORLD RECORD' and 'POLE VAULT FINAL' on a digital banner, with the track branded by 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS'. An upbeat electronic music track began as athletes, including Duplantis in blue and red, Lightfoot in green, and others in national team jackets, entered, accompanied by crowd noise and an announcer highlighting the 6.19 world record and introducing the 13 athletes. Specific introductions included Lillefosse of Norway, Commonwealth champion Curtis Marshalls, and World Student Games medalist Torben Blech, who were seen warming up. The starting lineup graphic listed Duplantis, Lillefosse, Lightfoot, Marshalls, Blech, Guttormsen, Zernikel, Broeders, Lavillenie, Vylon, Collet, and Braz. Guttormsen, in a purple jacket, was highlighted as a US collegiate champion indoors, Zernikel in a blue jacket as from Germany, Ben Broeders in a yellow jacket as a national record holder, Valentin Lavillenie in a blue jacket as a former European indoor silver medalist, and Thibaut Collet in a yellow jacket as a European Under 23 bronze medalist. The announcer then referred to "five big boys ready to go toe-to-toe." Vylon, in a yellow jacket and later an orange hoodie, was identified as Manoah Flune, an Olympic bronze medalist. Thiago Braz da Silva, in a teal jacket, was recalled for his 2016 Rio championship win. KC Lightfoot, in a green then blue jacket, was described as joint fourth in the Olympics and third best this year. Finally, Chris Neilson, in a blue jacket, was introduced as an Olympic silver medalist and a regular member of the 6-meter club. The athletes lined up on the red and blue track, branded with 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS', under dynamic lighting and on screens displaying animated pole vault silhouettes, with the scoreboard reading 'MEN'S POLE VAULT FINAL' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22', contributing to an electric atmosphere filled with crowd sounds and continuous background music.

Lightfoot.description: Athlete in a green jacket, later a blue jacket with a U.S. flag patch, seen clapping and smiling, identified by the announcer as KC Lightfoot, who was joint fourth in the Olympics last year and is the third best vaulter in the world this year.

event_blocks.summary: In a darkened indoor stadium illuminated by vibrant purple and blue lighting, the Men's Pole Vault Final commenced at the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22, with overhead screens displaying the event title and a digital scoreboard listing competitors. Armand Duplantis, the Swedish Olympic champion and world record holder, was introduced as the overwhelming favorite, noted for his perfect record in 2022. KC Lightfoot of the USA, wearing a maroon sleeveless top, successfully cleared the opening height of 5.45m with ease, described as making it look "very simple." Kurtis Marschall of Australia, in a green and yellow top, also successfully cleared the height on his second attempt after an initial struggle. Ben Broeders of Belgium, the national record holder, executed a clean vault at 5.45m, described as "very cool and calm." Valentin Lavillenie of France, the younger brother of Renaud, also safely cleared 5.45m. Menno Vloon of the Netherlands, a bronze medalist in Tokyo, then successfully vaulted 5.45m, described as a "brilliant bronze" and a "really powerful young man." Thibaut Collet of France, the European Under 23 Bronze medalist, also cleared 5.45m with a "very confident piece of vaulting." Christopher Nilsen of the USA, ranked number two and holder of a new USA indoor record, entered the competition at 5.45m, clearing it "absolutely miles clear" and bouncing "straight up to his feet," with commentators noting his potential to pressure Duplantis. The track was branded with 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS', and the atmosphere was intense, underscored by dynamic lighting, large digital screens, and crowd noise.

event_blocks.summary: The Men's Pole Vault Final at the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22 commenced with athletes like Marschall, in an 'AUSTRALIA' top, successfully clearing heights, while Zernikel faced early challenges. Duplantis, representing 'SWEDEN' in a bright yellow top, demonstrated intense concentration and precision, executing powerful vaults under dynamic purple and blue stadium lighting. His early clearances, including at 5.60m, were described as 'comfortable' and 'effortless,' with commentators noting his status as the world record holder. Competitors like Lillefosse of 'NORWAY' and Lightfoot of the 'USA' also vied for top positions, with the 'SEIKO' and 'ASICS' branded track providing a fast surface. Duplantis's performance, including a clearance at 5.85m, solidified his lead, with commentators highlighting his calm demeanor and confidence. Christopher Nilsen of the 'USA', an Olympic silver medalist, also performed strongly, clearing 5.90m to take the lead at one point, while Thiago Braz da Silva of 'BRASIL' and Valentin Lavillenie of 'FRANCE' contended for podium spots. Lavillenie, the Rio Olympic champion, eventually finished fourth after failing to clear 5.85m on his third attempt, while Braz secured third place. Duplantis ultimately retook the lead, with the competition intensifying as the bar was raised to 5.95m, with Duplantis, Nilsen, and Braz remaining in contention for the gold medal.

The segment opens at the Men's Pole Vault Final of the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22, with a male commentator detailing current clearances at 5.60m and 5.85m, noting an athlete passed at 5.90m, and the bar is now set at 5.95m. event_blocks.summary: Christopher Nilsen, in his red and blue USA top, executed a pole vault attempt at the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22, clearing 5.95m after two failures, while Thiago Braz, the former Olympic champion, also had two failures at the same height before delivering a clean vault on his third attempt, earning him second place with 5.95m behind Mondo Duplantis. Commentators noted Duplantis was close to gold and a potential world record, having brushed the bar with his chest on a previous attempt, and that he had only jumped three times, keeping his legs fresh. Braz celebrated his successful vault with clenched fists and a yell, later interacting with a young fan. Nilsen, described as playing catch-up, had a final attempt at 5.95m, with the scoreboard updating to reflect his 5.90m clearance, ultimately securing him the bronze medal. Duplantis then successfully cleared 6 meters, with commentators anticipating a potential world record attempt. He continued to clear heights, ultimately reaching 6.05m and then attempting a world record of 6.20m. Braz, after securing silver, performed a celebratory backflip. Duplantis, described as fearless and flawless, was confirmed as the World Indoor Champion, having cleared 6.05m, and later attempted 6.20m for a new world record, which he successfully cleared, solidifying his dominance in the event.

event_blocks.summary: In the Men's Pole Vault Final at the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22, Armand Duplantis, representing Sweden, delivered a spectacular performance, breaking his own world indoor record by clearing 6.20m. The atmosphere in the brightly lit stadium, awash in purple and blue hues, was electric as Duplantis, clad in a yellow 'SWEDEN' top, executed his final, record-breaking vault. Spectators erupted in thunderous cheers, waving Swedish flags and capturing the historic moment on their phones, while commentators exclaimed in disbelief and awe, calling Duplantis a "rockstar" who "delivered" under pressure. Following his triumph, Duplantis celebrated warmly with fans in the stands, including his mother, a blonde woman in a blue jacket, and his father, before respectfully shaking hands with fellow competitors like Thiago Braz of Brazil and Christopher Nilsen of the USA. The digital scoreboard confirmed Duplantis's world record, with Braz securing silver at 5.95m and Nilsen taking bronze at 5.90m. The event concluded with commentators praising the "amazing job" of the Serbian hosts and highlighting Duplantis's "fearless and flawless" performance, marking a "world indoor championship that will long, long live in the memory."

raw_segments: The video opens with Duplantis, the athlete in a bright yellow sleeveless top with 'SWEDEN' across the chest and sponsor logos 'CRAFT' and 'BAUHAUS', walking calmly with his yellow and purple pole vault pole, adjusting his hair and engaging in a focused conversation with a coach in a black shirt and face mask. On-screen text and a digital scoreboard confirm the setting as the Men's Pole Vault Final at the World Athletics Indoor Championships Belgrade 22, with Duplantis leading at 6.05m. An announcer's voice builds anticipation, asking if "the final word" will go to "Mondo Duplantis for a world record," calling him "the rockstar." Duplantis then sprints down the red and blue track, plants the pole, and executes a powerful vault, clearing the bar set at 6.20m as confirmed by the 'MONDO' digital display. As he lands safely on the blue mat, the crowd erupts in thunderous cheers, and the announcer exclaims excitedly, "And he's done it! He's done it! I cannot believe it!" Duplantis walks off the mat with a satisfied expression, approaching the stands to celebrate with fans. He hugs a blonde woman in a blue jacket, raises his arm in triumph, and interacts warmly with spectators, including a man in an orange shirt who records the moment on his phone. The atmosphere is electric, with Swedish flags waving and fans capturing the moment on their devices, as the announcer declares, "The curtain comes down on an absolutely incredible, incredible three days in Belgrade," noting that "everybody is on their feet" and "the entire stadium is now jumping." The announcer praises the "Serbian hosts" for an "amazing job," concluding that "Mondo Duplantis delivered" as if "perfectly to script." The bright lighting highlights the vibrant arena colors, and a digital timer near the vaulting area counts down from 2:07 to 2:05, capturing the emotional high of a successful vault and the athlete's connection with his supporters.

The segment opens with a commentator declaring Duplantis's triumph, stating he has achieved "not just the gold, but a world record," marking a "world indoor championship that will long, long live in the memory." As the crowd's cheers resonate, Duplantis, wearing his bright yellow 'SWEDEN' top and blue shorts, walks along the red and blue track, waving to the cheering fans who wave Swedish flags and record the moment on their phones. The commentator emphasizes, "World class athletics is back and the rockstar Mondo Duplantis is the man who has delivered once again." Duplantis, carrying his yellow and purple pole, approaches the stands, where he shares a heartfelt hug with a blonde woman in a blue jacket, identified by the commentator as his "mom," before greeting a man in a dark blue 'BAUHAUS' shirt, referred to as his "dad," with a firm handshake and brief conversation. The stadium is brightly lit with purple and blue hues, and the digital scoreboard confirms 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22' and 'MEN'S POLE VAULT | FINAL', while a Mondo digital display shows 'HEIGHT 6.20'. Duplantis continues his celebration by raising his arm in triumph, acknowledging the electric atmosphere, as a man in an orange shirt records and a photographer in green captures images. The commentator concludes, praising "a young man who simply knows no bounds," having set "another world record, another great moment," as Mondo Duplantis "signs us off in style here in Serbia," walking confidently back towards the vaulting area, pole still in hand, amidst the sustained roar of the crowd.

The video opens with athlete Mondo Duplantis, in a bright yellow 'SWEDEN' top, walking confidently with his yellow and purple pole vault pole, adjusting his hair, and briefly conversing with a coach or teammate in a dark blue 'BAUHAUS' shirt, shaking hands. The atmosphere is electric, with spectators waving Swedish flags and the digital scoreboard displaying 'WORLD RECORD'. A male commentator exclaims, "believe what Mondo Duplantis has just done," noting he "had to wait until the very end... to try and deliver." The camera shifts to a wide-angle view of the red and blue track, branded 'TDK' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS', with a digital timer counting down. Duplantis sprints powerfully, plants the pole, and launches into a graceful arc, clearing the bar at 6.20m, confirmed by the 'MONDO' display. The commentator declares, "This whole place is bouncing!" and notes, "You could be forgiven for thinking he's done it for Serbia, rather than Sweden," highlighting the local support amidst the constant crowd cheering and applause. Duplantis lands safely on the 'WORLD ATHLETICS INDOOR CHAMPIONSHIPS BELGRADE 22' mat, raising his arms in triumph as the 'WORLD RECORD' message flashes. The male commentator adds, "It wasn't the tidiest clearance, but it doesn't matter," and addresses a co-commentator, "Hannah England, what a way... for these three days to come to an end. The very moment we thought it was over, and we finish with a world record. That is incredible!" Hannah England then adds, "Oh, but that is poetry in motion. His first attempt didn't look that good, his second attempt looked even worse, and look at the way he soars over that world record attempt." Duplantis walks off the mat with a satisfied expression, approaches the stands to hug a blonde woman in a blue jacket, and raises his arm in victory, while a man in an orange shirt records the moment. Braz, in a green and yellow 'BRASIL' top, approaches Duplantis to shake hands respectfully, and a young fan receives an item from Braz, all under the vibrant stadium lighting.

The segment opens with Duplantis, clad in his bright yellow 'SWEDEN' top, executing a successful pole vault, clearing the bar at 6.20m as confirmed by the 'MONDO' digital display. A female commentator's excited voice exclaims, "This was it, this was the final moment of the championships," as Duplantis lands on the mat, raising his arms in triumph to the sound of a cheering crowd. She continues, highlighting the anticipation, "Were they going to witness a world record?" He walks off with a satisfied expression, and the scene transitions to the stands where he is warmly embraced by a blonde woman in a blue jacket, likely a family member, amidst continued cheers. The commentator emphasizes, "Look what that means to Armand Duplantis. Embraces all round for his friends and family and his loved ones. Armand Duplantis delivering under pressure." The camera then cuts to a wider view of the brightly lit stadium, accented with purple and blue hues, showing Duplantis walking alongside other athletes. Braz, in his green and yellow 'BRASIL' top, approaches to shake Duplantis's hand before walking away, later seen giving a small item to a young fan in a blue jacket. A male commentator takes over, noting, "He would have loved to put on a show for the crowd," and points out the Jamaican team reacting to the world record attempt. The video opens with a wide-angle, high-angle shot of the brightly lit indoor athletics stadium during the Men's Pole Vault Final at the World Indoor Championships Belgrade 22. The arena glows with vibrant purple and blue hues, illuminating the red and blue track, which is clearly branded with 'TDK' and 'WORLD ATHLETICS' on its barriers. The vaulting area is meticulously set up with the landing mat, bar, and timing devices, while a Mondo Digital Display shows 'DEPTH 0 HEIGHT 6.20', indicating the current bar height. The digital scoreboard and on-screen graphics prominently display 'MEN'S POLE VAULT | FINAL' and the current results, showing Armand Duplantis (SWE) in first place with a world record (WR) of 6.20m, followed by Thiago Braz (BRA) at 5.95m (AR) and Christopher Nilsen (USA) at 5.90m. A Starting Lineup Graphic also lists the top competitors, including Duplantis, Nilsen, and Braz. Throughout this, a male commentator exclaims with excitement, "What a finish! What a finish!" and confirms, "Mondo Duplantis breaks his own world indoor record," praising him as "fearless and flawless." He also announces, "Thiago Braz the silver, Chris Neilson the bronze," before concluding that "the applause and the history books belong to Mondo." The camera remains static, capturing the electric atmosphere as spectators fill the stands, some waving flags, including a prominent Swedish flag, reflecting the commentator's observation that "people are stunned here in Belgrade." The scene transitions to a close-up of the scoreboard, emphasizing the final results, before returning to the wide shot of the stadium, which, despite no athletes actively competing in this segment, is charged with anticipation and celebration, particularly for Duplantis, who is later shown celebrating with a fist pump and hugging a woman in a blue jacket in the stands. The presence of officials, photographers, and fans further enhances the professional and festive atmosphere of the event.
